title: From Punchlines to Predictions: A Metric to Assess LLM Performance in Identifying Humor in Stand-Up Comedy
research_questions: How can LLM performance in extracting humorous punchlines from stand-up comedy transcripts be scored while accounting for the subjectivity of humor?
How well do different LLMs identify humorous quotes from stand-up comedy transcripts in zero-shot prompting settings?
How do prompt variations, persona prompts, humor-preference prompts, comedian personas, and audience-demographic prompts affect humor detection performance?
How do LLM humor-detection outputs compare with human evaluators, and how much do humans and models agree on which transcript lines are funny?

datasets_used.name: Open Mic dataset
datasets_used.size: 51 transcripts with an average word length of 270 words and length of 106 seconds

methods_proposed.name: Humor detection metric with fuzzy string matching, vector embedding, and subspace similarity modules
methods_proposed.summary: The metric prompts an LLM to extract humorous quotes from a stand-up transcript and compares the predicted quote set with ground-truth punchlines derived from audience laughter timestamps and forced alignment. It provides three alternative scoring modules: fuzzy string matching over strings, sentence-embedding similarity over quote vectors, and subspace similarity over sets of outputs across prompt variations.

humor_theories.detail: The paper lists Superiority Theory as one of three linguistic theories explaining humor, where humor arises from feeling superior to others.
The paper lists Relief Theory as one of three linguistic theories explaining humor, where humor releases psychological tension.
The paper describes stand-up comedy as mirroring Incongruity Theory, where comedians create an expectation through a set-up and subvert it with the punchline.

evaluation_methods.detail: The proposed metric scores model outputs against ground-truth humorous quotes using one of three modules: fuzzy string matching, sentence embeddings, or subspace similarity; overgeneration is penalized with alpha = 0.1.
Ground truth humorous quotes are derived from laughter during stand-up performances using a laughter detection model, laughter timestamps, forced alignment, audio, and transcripts.
11 naive participants from various cultural backgrounds, all aged 20 to 30 years, marked sentences as funny or not on 6 transcripts from well-known comedians.
Human answers were evaluated against the ground truth using the fuzzy string matching module; Percentage Agreement was used for human-human and human-machine agreement.

headline_results.finding: Across the 51-transcript model comparison, DeepSeek-V3 achieved the highest embedding score reported in Table 1.
ChatGPT-4o achieved the highest fuzzy string matching score among the leading models in Table 1, but its embedding score dropped sharply.
The highest subspace score reported in Table 1 was achieved by Gemma 2b-it.
Human evaluators scored below the leading LLMs when their answers were evaluated against the ground truth with the fuzzy string matching module.
Human evaluators showed high agreement with each other across the 6-transcript human evaluation subset.
Human-machine agreement averaged 59.9%, with Gemma 2b-instruct and Gemma 2 9b-instruct tied for the highest agreement and ChatGPT-4o the lowest.

key_findings: The paper introduces a modular metric for stand-up humor detection that can score LLM outputs using fuzzy string matching, sentence-embedding similarity, or subspace similarity.
On 51 Open Mic transcripts, leading models still performed only around the 50% range, with DeepSeek-V3 reaching 51.6% on the embedding module and ChatGPT-4o reaching 48.9% on the fuzzy module.
Human evaluators scored 40.7% against the laughter-derived ground truth using the fuzzy string module, so the leading LLMs outperformed humans under this text-based metric.

Prompt engineering generally did not produce meaningful improvements: Prompt 2 scored 31.2% for Gemma 2b-instruct versus the original 30.1%, while persona, humor-type, comedian, and demographic prompts mostly reduced scores.
Human-human Percentage Agreement was high at 86.7% on the 6-transcript subset, but human-machine agreement averaged only 59.9%, showing that metric score and agreement with humans are not the same.
ChatGPT-4o had a strong fuzzy score but the lowest human-machine agreement, 28.7%, which the paper uses to argue that agreement rate and metric performance can diverge.
limitations: Ground truth is derived from laughter timestamps, and the paper assumes that the sentence preceding laughter is humorous, so the most humorous part of a joke may be misattributed.
The ground truth does not differentiate laughter magnitude.
The laughter detection model used a minimum laughter length of 0.2 seconds and a minimum probability threshold of 0.5, which may have missed subtler audience reactions.
The study relies on text-based analysis of stand-up comedy, omitting tone, timing, body language, and other live-performance cues.
The human evaluation participants were all within an age range of 20 to 30 years, which the paper notes could narrow the diversity of humor interpretations.
safety_ethics_notes: The paper states that the stand-up comedy audio recordings and transcripts may contain humor that some may find offensive or politically incorrect, and that the content was analyzed solely for research purposes without endorsement of any particular viewpoint.

human_samples.population: Live audiences in the Open Mic stand-up comedy dataset performances used to derive laughter-based ground truth.
human_samples.observations: Laughter timestamps were extracted from accompanying audio for 51 randomly selected transcripts; forced alignment mapped laughter time frames to transcript sentences, and the sentence preceding laughter was treated as the humorous ground truth.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report audience counts or demographics. The laughter detector used default minimum laughter length 0.2 seconds and probability threshold 0.5.

human_samples.population: Naive raters across various cultural backgrounds, all aged 20 to 30 years.
human_samples.observations: Each participant received 6 transcripts from well-known comedians, split into sentences, and marked each sentence as funny or not. Their answers were used for human-human Percentage Agreement, human-machine Percentage Agreement, and a fuzzy-string score against ground truth.
human_samples.detail: The paper reports 86.7% average human-human PA and 40.7% human score against the laughter-derived ground truth using the fuzzy string module.

human_samples.population: Stand-up comedians represented in the Open Mic dataset; the human-evaluation subset used transcripts from Ali Wong, Anthony Jeselnik, Hasan Minhaj, Jimmy Yang, Joe List, and John Mulaney.
human_samples.observations: 51 stand-up transcripts were randomly selected for the main model evaluation; 6 transcripts from well-known comedians were used for human evaluation.
human_samples.detail: The paper does not report the number of distinct comedians in the 51-transcript set or creator demographics.

judges.detail: Audience laughter in the original stand-up recordings is the substantive signal for the ground-truth humorous quotes/punchlines.

judges.name: Gillick (2017) laughter detection model plus forced alignment
judges.detail: The laughter detection model extracted laughter timestamps from audio; forced alignment mapped timestamps to transcript sentences. The study assumes the sentence preceding detected laughter is humorous.

judges.name: Proposed fuzzy string matching module
judges.detail: Uses Levenshtein/fuzzy string similarity between model-predicted quote lists and ground-truth quote lists, selects the best match for each ground truth, assigns 0 to unmatched ground truths, and applies an overgeneration penalty with alpha = 0.1.

judges.name: Proposed vector embedding module
judges.detail: Uses Sentence Transformers sentence embeddings to compare semantic similarity between predicted and ground-truth quotes, with the same averaging and overgeneration-penalty procedure as the fuzzy module.

judges.name: Proposed subspace similarity module
judges.detail: Uses feature-vector subspaces formed from model outputs and ground truths across multiple instruction variations; PCA/SVD and canonical-angle similarity produce the subspace score.

judges.name: 11 naive raters
judges.detail: Marked sentence-split transcripts as funny or not. A quote was treated as funny for human-machine PA if a majority of raters voted for it.

judges.name: Percentage Agreement
judges.detail: Used to compute agreement between human evaluators and between human majority labels and LLM outputs.

generation_selection.scope: Prompt 1 model comparison on 51 Open Mic transcripts
generation_selection.n: One output list per model/transcript is described; repeated candidates are not reported.
generation_selection.detail: Models were zero-shot prompted with a transcript and asked to extract humorous lines/punchlines. The paper does not describe generating multiple candidate responses and selecting the best.

generation_selection.scope: Prompt-engineering, persona, humor-type, comedian-persona, and audience-demographic prompt experiments
generation_selection.n: One output list per prompt condition/transcript/model is described; repeated candidates are not reported.
generation_selection.detail: All reported prompt variants were evaluated with the fuzzy string module. Prompts 2–4 were generated using ChatGPT from a transcript and ground truth, but the paper does not describe best-of-N output selection.

generation_selection.n: Multiple instruction variations are used to form subspaces, but the exact number of variations is not reported.
generation_selection.detail: Outputs from multiple prompt variations are aggregated into a subspace for scoring; this is not best-of-N selection because no candidate output is selected as the final answer.

model_provenance.name: Gemma 2b-it / Gemma 2b-instruct
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Google's Gemma with 2-billion parameters and labeled in tables as Gemma 2b-it or Gemma 2b-instruct. Exact checkpoint, release, and execution environment are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemma 2 9b-it / Gemma 2 9b-instruct
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Google's Gemma 2 with 9-billion parameters and labeled in tables as Gemma 2 9b-it or Gemma 2 9b-instruct. Exact checkpoint and execution environment are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Gemma 2 27-b parameter model
model_provenance.detail: The paper discusses a 27-billion-parameter Gemma 2 model in the Gemma 2-it family evaluation, but the exact model label/checkpoint and table values are not fully reported in text.

model_provenance.name: Phi 3-Mini 3.8b-it / Phi3-Mini 3.8b-instruct
model_provenance.detail: Reported as Microsoft's Phi 3-Mini with 3.8-billion parameters; exact checkpoint and execution environment are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Llama 3.1 8B Instruct / Llama 3 8b-it
model_provenance.detail: The prose says Meta's Llama 3.1 with 8-billion parameters; tables label it Llama 3 8b-it or Llama 3 8b-instruct. Exact checkpoint and execution environment are not reported.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT-4o
model_provenance.detail: Evaluated as a model in the main comparison and comedian-persona prompt test. No API snapshot/version, access mode, or inference date is reported.

model_provenance.name: ChatGPT
model_provenance.detail: Used to generate Prompts 2–4 by providing a transcript and ground truth and asking for an instruction. The paper does not state whether this was ChatGPT-4o or another ChatGPT model.

model_provenance.name: Claude 3.5 Sonnet
model_provenance.detail: Footnote states experiments were conducted in December 2024. Exact Claude snapshot/API version and access mode are not reported.

model_provenance.name: DeepSeek-V3
model_provenance.detail: Footnote states the experiment was conducted in January 2025. Exact service/checkpoint snapshot and access mode are not reported.

model_provenance.name: Sentence Transformers embedding model
model_provenance.detail: Used in the vector embedding scoring module; the exact Sentence Transformers model is not named.

model_provenance.name: Gillick (2017) laughter detection model
model_provenance.detail: Used to extract laughter timestamps from audio. The referenced GitHub resource was accessed 2024-09-26; the paper reports default thresholds but not a model version/checkpoint.

model_provenance.name: Forced alignment system
model_provenance.detail: Used to map audio/laughter timestamps to transcript sentences, but the exact forced-alignment tool/model and settings are not reported.

inference_budgets.scope: LLM quote-extraction experiments for model comparison and prompt engineering
inference_budgets.calls_per_item: One prompt with a transcript per model/prompt condition is described; exact call counts, reruns, or failed calls are not reported.
inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: One output list is scored per model/prompt condition; no alternate candidates are reported.
inference_budgets.compute_or_cost: All experiments ran in less than a day.
inference_budgets.detail: The setting is described as zero-shot prompting. The main dataset has 51 transcripts averaging 270 words and 106 seconds.

inference_budgets.calls_per_item: Multiple instruction variations per transcript/model are required, but the exact number is not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: Multiple outputs are used to form model and ground-truth subspaces; q/r dimensions and full prompt-variation details are not reported.

inference_budgets.candidates_per_item: Three prompt variants are reported, but the number of ChatGPT calls/samples is not reported.
inference_budgets.detail: ChatGPT was given a transcript and ground truth and asked what instruction would extract that quote list.

inference_budgets.sampling_parameters: Minimum laughter length 0.2 seconds and minimum probability threshold 0.5, described as default values.
inference_budgets.detail: Forced-alignment settings and the number of detected laughter events are not reported.
The paper does not report the exact embedding model, embedding dimensionality, batching, or compute budget.

human_baselines.description: 11 naive raters marked sentence-split transcripts as funny/not. Human answers scored 40.7% against the laughter-derived ground truth using the fuzzy string module; average human-human Percentage Agreement was 86.7%. Majority human labels were used for human-machine PA.
human_baselines.detail: The human and model tasks are related and use the same transcript source, but conditions are not materially matched: humans made binary judgments over pre-segmented sentences, while LLMs generated free-form quote lists. The headline model comparison uses 51 transcripts, while the human evaluation uses 6 transcripts; model metric scores on exactly that 6-transcript subset are not separately reported.

contamination_risk.basis: The Open Mic dataset is from Mittal et al. (2021), preceding the 2024–2025 models evaluated or run here, and the human-evaluation subset uses well-known comedians whose routines/transcripts may be present in pretraining data. The paper reports no decontamination test, private held-out items, or training-data audit. Risk is moderated because the transcript is provided at inference and labels are derived from laughter timestamps, but this is still a public legacy benchmark evaluated with later LLMs.

llm_judge_dependence.human_validation: Human raters provide a separate baseline and agreement analysis, not validation of an LLM judge.
llm_judge_dependence.detail: Headline scores are determined by live-audience-laughter-derived ground truth and automatic similarity/PA metrics. ChatGPT was used to suggest prompt variants, but no LLM is used to judge model outputs or assign evaluation labels.

reporting_gaps: Exact model checkpoints, API snapshots, and release identifiers are not reported for evaluated LLMs; only Claude 3.5 Sonnet's December 2024 and DeepSeek-V3's January 2025 experiment dates are footnoted.
Access mode and execution environment for all LLMs are not reported, including whether proprietary systems were accessed by API or hosted interface and whether open-weight models were run locally.
Sampling and decoding parameters such as temperature, top_p, max tokens, stop sequences, and random seeds are not reported.
Exact number of calls, reruns, failed generations, and output-regeneration policy are not reported.
The exact Sentence Transformers embedding model, embedding dimensionality, and inference settings are not reported.
The forced-alignment tool/model and settings are not named.
The random selection procedure, seed, and full identity of the 51 selected transcripts are not reported in the paper.
Counts of ground-truth punchlines, detected laughter events, transcript sentences, and model overgenerated quotes are not reported.
Live-audience participant counts, demographics, geography, and language information are not reported.
Human-rater recruitment method, geography, gender distribution, compensation, language proficiency, exclusion criteria, task ordering, and blinding are not reported.
Model naming is inconsistent in places, e.g. Llama 3.1 versus Llama 3 and Gemma 2b-it versus Gemma 2b-instruct.
Subspace-module prompt variations, exact count of variations, and q/r dimensional settings are not fully reported.
Prompt generation with ChatGPT lacks exact model version, inference date, number of calls/samples, and selection criteria for Prompts 2–4.
The paper does not report LLM fuzzy-string metric scores specifically on the same 6 transcripts used for the human baseline.
